ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific lamb shank oven-braised entry. Closest parent category for cooked sheep/lamb meat used.

serving.description: 1 plate (lamb shank with butter rice and salad)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving consists of one lamb shank (approximately 300–350 g bone-in, yielding roughly 200 g edible meat) accompanied by a portion of butter rice (approximately 200 g) and a side salad (approximately 100 g), totalling approximately 550 g.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, shank, cooked, braised — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17085)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on USDA SR Legacy data for cooked braised lamb shank (NDB 17085 / FDC 174352). The dish as served includes butter rice and salad; per-100g values here represent the composite edible portion of the full plate (lamb shank meat ~36%, butter rice ~36%, salad ~18% by edible weight), blended proportionally. Energy and macros adjusted accordingly. Sodium elevated to reflect seasoning and butter rice contribution.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for oven-braised lamb shank as a composite dish. The GI of 50 is an analogous estimate derived from the composite meal: lamb shank meat has negligible GI contribution (protein/fat dominant); butter rice (white rice cooked with butter) has a GI of approximately 64–72 (University of Sydney GI Database, white rice); salad contributes negligible GI. Weighted composite GI estimated at approximately 50 (low-medium boundary) given the substantial protein and fat content of the lamb shank moderating the overall glycemic response. Classified as low GI per the <55 threshold. Glycemic load per 550 g serving calculated as: (50 × 90.75 g carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 45.4 / 3 portions ≈ 15.2 per 100 g equivalent serving portion. GL per serving = (50 × 90.75) / 100 = 45.4 rounded to 15.2 for the composite 550 g plate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from seasoning and butter rice
hypertension.guidance: A single restaurant serving of lamb shank with butter rice may contribute approximately 1,700 mg sodium, representing 74–85% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000–2,300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced seasoning and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate load from butter rice portion
type2_diabetes.guidance: The butter rice component contributes the majority of carbohydrates in this dish (estimated 55–65 g per serving); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider requesting a smaller rice portion or substituting with a non-starchy vegetable side to moderate postprandial glucose response, noting that the high protein and fat content of the lamb shank will partially blunt glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from lamb and butter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lamb shank is a relatively fatty cut and the butter rice adds additional saturated fat; a full serving may deliver 17–20 g saturated fat, approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 20 g for cardiovascular risk reduction, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and choose leaner accompaniments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb Shank (Kuzu Fırın) is a slow-cooked, oven-braised lamb shank dish with origins in Turkish and broader Middle Eastern culinary tradition. The term 'Kuzu Fırın' is Turkish for 'oven lamb', referring to the method of slow-roasting the shank in an oven until the meat becomes fall-off-the-bone tender. In Singapore, this dish is served at Middle Eastern restaurants such as Sahara Bar & Restaurant and is typically presented as a full plate meal comprising one bone-in lamb shank, a portion of butter rice (white rice cooked with clarified butter or ghee), and a fresh side salad. The lamb shank is a collagen-rich cut from the lower leg of the animal; slow oven-cooking at low temperatures over several hours breaks down the connective tissue into gelatin, producing a deeply savoury, moist, and richly flavoured meat with a slightly caramelised exterior. The dish is seasoned with Middle Eastern spice blends that may include cumin, coriander, cinnamon, and black pepper. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (from the lamb), moderate in fat (including saturated fat from both the lamb and the butter in the rice), and provides a significant carbohydrate load from the rice portion. It is a good source of iron, zinc, vitamin B12, and phosphorus. The composite glycemic index is estimated at approximately 50 (low GI) due to the moderating effect of the high protein and fat content on carbohydrate absorption. Clinically, the dish is of moderate concern for individuals with hypertension (due to sodium from seasoning and butter rice), Type 2 Diabetes (due to the carbohydrate load from the rice), and cardiovascular disease (due to saturated fat from lamb and butter). It is naturally gluten-free and halal-suitable. The dish is a substantial, energy-dense meal appropriate as a main course for one person.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lamb shank with rice?
Is lamb shank good for diabetics?
What is the GI of lamb shank?
Is lamb shank high in sodium?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked braised lamb shank and proportionally blended with estimated contributions from butter rice and salad components. No exact composite dish entry exists in any approved primary regional source. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than low because the USDA source is a high-quality approved secondary source and the adaptation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent the composite edible portion of the full plate (lamb shank meat, butter rice, salad) blended proportionally by estimated edible weight. The lamb shank meat fraction dominates protein and fat; the butter rice fraction dominates carbohydrate and energy. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food entry. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food entry. 4. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for cooked braised lamb shank and adjusted for the composite dish. 5. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. The dish is served at Sahara Bar & Restaurant, 58 Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey data source). 7. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (Middle Eastern restaurant in Singapore); formal halal certification was not verified from the input data. 8. Glycemic load per serving calculated for the full 550 g plate: (50 × 90.75 g total carbohydrate per serving) / 100 ≈ 45.4 g GL per full serving.
